title: Writing Guide
text:
Preface
This guide grew out of frustration with a pattern I kept seeing in my own lab. Talented early career researchers would produce manuscript drafts that were theoretically exhaustive but argumentatively weak. Every relevant theory got a paragraph. Every related concept got a mention. The papers were comprehensive and hard to read.
The problem was not knowledge. My students knew the literature well, sometimes too well. The problem was a set of unwritten rules: how to decide which theories are load-bearing for your argument and which are not, how to write for a reader who needs to be convinced rather than impressed, and how to survive a review process that sometimes rewards the wrong things.
I work in cognitive psychology, at the intersection of perception, memory, and narrative cognition. It is a field rich in theory and, at times, poor in argument. We have more frameworks than we have crucial tests between them. I have watched gifted researchers spend months writing introductions that cover the landscape rather than staking a position in it, and I have read the reviews that follow: specific, detailed, and asking for three more theories.
This problem has become more visible with the arrival of AI writing tools. Ask an AI assistant to improve your introduction and it will suggest theories you have not mentioned. The writing comes back polished and bloated, and it sounds authoritative enough that the missing argument becomes harder to notice.
This guide is my attempt to make some of those unwritten rules explicit. It is not a comprehensive textbook on scientific writing; there are better books for that. It is a practical guide for researchers who already know their science and are struggling to turn it into a paper that works. The final chapter addresses AI writing tools directly, not to discourage their use, but to help you use them carefully.
The problem this guide addresses is most acute in theory-rich fields like psychology, where phenomena attract multiple competing accounts and reviewing culture reinforces citation of all of them. In fields with tighter theoretical consensus, or where data more reliably rule out competing accounts, the pressure is lower. But psychology is not one of those fields, and neither are most of the disciplines adjacent to it.
The diagnosis is not new. Oberauer and Lewandowsky (2019) have argued that psychology’s theory crisis stems less from bad data than from a weak connection between theories and the predictions researchers actually test. The concept of strong inference, introduced by the physicist John Platt (1964), points toward a remedy. Platt observed that some fields were advancing far faster than others, and traced the difference to a specific habit of mind: devising competing hypotheses and designing experiments that could exclude at least one of them. He was writing for bench scientists. But his core insight applies directly to the problem this guide addresses. A theoretical framing that cannot say which result would count against which theory is not doing scientific work. The filters in this guide are, among other things, a way of asking Platt’s question at the level of the manuscript: does this theory belong here because it can lose, or because it feels relevant?
The examples throughout are drawn from event cognition research, which is where most of my own work lives. But the principles apply wherever you write a paper that makes a theoretical claim. If you work in an adjacent field, the norms around theoretical framing may differ from what is described here; treat the specific advice as a starting point rather than a prescription.
If you are a PhD student preparing your first manuscript, a postdoc revising a rejected paper, or a supervisor looking for something to hand your students, this is for you.




Oberauer, K., & Lewandowsky, S. (2019). Addressing the theory crisis in psychology. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 26(5), 1596–1618. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-019-01645-2


Platt, J. R. (1964). Strong Inference. Science, 146(3642), 347–353. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.146.3642.347

title: 1  The Problem
text:
1.1 The theory overload problem
When researchers sit down to write, they face a field that has accumulated theories faster than it has retired them. The rational response — mention everything that might be relevant — produces manuscripts that are theoretically exhaustive and argumentatively weak. A paper that should make one clear argument instead surveys a landscape of loosely related theories, hedges every claim, and leaves the reader wondering what the point was.
The rational response, that is, mention everything that might be relevant, produces manuscripts that are theoretically exhaustive and argumentatively weak. The cause is structural, not individual.
Psychology — and cognitive psychology in particular — is a theory-rich field. For almost any phenomenon you study, there are multiple accounts, frameworks, and models that are at least partially relevant. Event cognition alone has produced a remarkable number of competing and overlapping theories: Event Segmentation Theory, Situation Models, Narrative Comprehension models, and more.
When a PhD student sits down to write, they face a genuine dilemma. They know this landscape well. They also know that reviewers are drawn from that same landscape — and that reviewers notice when their preferred theory is not discussed.
The rational response is to mention everything. The result is a manuscript that mentions everything and argues nothing.

title: 1  The Problem
text:
1.2 Where it comes from
The problem has three interlocking sources:
Theory proliferation. Psychology has accumulated theories faster than it has retired them. Many theories overlap substantially but use different terminology, which makes them look more distinct than they are.
Reviewing culture. Reviewers sometimes treat citation of their preferred framework as a condition of acceptance. This is rarely stated explicitly, but early-career researchers learn quickly that omitting a theory can sink a paper.
Writer response. Faced with these pressures, writers rationally expand their theoretical framing to pre-empt criticism. This makes each paper slightly worse, which makes the overall problem slightly worse.
These three forces form a feedback loop. No single actor is to blame, and no single fix works in isolation. But the writer is the one who can act — and that is what this guide is about.

title: 2  Finding your Anchor
text:
2.1 What makes a good anchor theory
Before you can decide which theories belong in your paper and which do not, you need to answer a more fundamental question: which theory is actually driving your study?
We call this your anchor theory. It is the theoretical account that is load-bearing for your hypotheses – the one you would have to abandon if your results came out differently. Every other theory in your manuscript will be judged relative to it.
Finding your anchor is harder than it sounds. Many studies grow organically from a lab tradition, a paradigm, or an interesting effect rather than from a single theoretical commitment. In those cases, the anchor is often implicit – everyone in the lab knows which theory motivates the work, but nobody has written it down explicitly. Writing it down is the first act of writing the paper.
A good anchor theory has three properties. First, you can derive your specific hypothesis directly from it – not loosely inspired by it, but derived from it. Second, it makes a concrete prediction about your specific manipulation and dependent variable, not just about the broader phenomenon. Third, if your results contradict it, something scientifically important has been learned.
A theory that predicts the same result regardless of what you found is background, not an anchor.
Sometimes finding the anchor theory is impossible because the study was not designed around one. The study was never built around a theoretical commitment, and no amount of good writing can fix that. Stop writing and talk to your collaborators before going further.

title: 2  Finding your Anchor
text:
TipThe anchor exercise



Before writing a single word of your introduction, complete this sentence:

This study tests the prediction of [theory] that [specific hypothesis], as operationalized by [dependent variable and manipulation].

If you can complete that sentence cleanly and specifically, you have your anchor. If you cannot – if the sentence feels vague, or if several theories seem equally plausible – you are not ready to write yet. Go back to your design and your data, and ask which theoretical account is most directly at stake.
Once you have your anchor, everything else follows. The filters in the next chapter are all answered relative to it.

title: 2  Finding your Anchor
text:
2.2 Preregistration and the anchor
Preregistering a study has become standard practice in many areas of psychology, and for good reason. It creates a public record of your theoretical commitment before you see the data, protecting against the temptation to adjust the framing retrospectively to fit the results – what Kerr (1998) called HARKing, Hypothesizing After Results are Known.
But preregistration only does this work if the registered hypothesis names the theory and the mechanism, not just the expected effect. A preregistration that states “we expect memory to be better at event boundaries” records a prediction without a reason. It does not specify which theory generates the prediction, what mechanism connects theory to outcome, or what result would count against it. Oberauer & Lewandowsky (2019) make this point directly: preregistering a hypothesis that is only loosely connected to a theory does not increase the credibility of that hypothesis, because the inferential link from theory to prediction was weak to begin with. Preregistration records your thinking. It cannot replace it.
In practice, experiments often run faster than the theoretical thinking is fully worked out. A preregistration written in the week before data collection captures the design, the expected effect, and the analysis plan – but not always the anchor. This is common. It is not a moral failing. It is what happens when the practical pressures of running a study outpace the slower work of theoretical clarification.
What it means for writing is this: having a preregistration does not mean the anchor exercise has been done. If your preregistration names an expected effect without naming the theory, the mechanism, and the specific prediction, the anchor exercise remains necessary. Doing it now, before writing, is how you supply what the preregistration should have contained. And the anchor sentence you produce is also the core of a stronger preregistration for your next study.

title: 2  Finding your Anchor
text:
2.3 Before you write: two checks
With your anchor in hand, two further steps prepare you to write. The first checks the anchor itself. The second maps the theoretical landscape around it. Both need to be done before you open a document and start drafting.






TipThe two-sentence check



Complete both sentences below. Write them down.
Sentence 1: Your anchor
My anchor theory is [theory], which predicts [specific hypothesis] in my study because [mechanism].
This sentence should be uncomfortable to write vaguely. If you find yourself writing “which predicts generally better performance” or “because of cognitive factors”, you have not yet found your anchor. A good anchor sentence names a specific mechanism and connects it to a specific outcome in your specific design.
A weak anchor sentence:

My anchor theory is Event Segmentation Theory, which predicts better memory in my study because of event boundaries.

A strong anchor sentence:

My anchor theory is Event Segmentation Theory, which predicts that memory for actions will be better at event boundaries than within events in my study, because boundary detection triggers an updating of the current event model, making boundary-adjacent information more accessible.

The difference is specificity. The strong version could only describe one study. The weak version could describe almost any study in event cognition.
Sentence 2: Your landscape
Other theories in the field that address [phenomenon] include [list]. They do not appear in this paper / appear only briefly because [reason for each].
This sentence forces you to be explicit about your exclusions before a reviewer forces you to be defensive about them. The reason for exclusion should always map onto one of the two filters: either the theory makes no differential prediction for this design, or it does not reach your dependent variable mechanistically.
A weak landscape sentence:

Other theories in the field that address event cognition include the Event-Indexing Model and the Structure Building Framework. They do not appear in this paper because they are not the focus of this study.

That reason – “not the focus” – is circular. It says nothing. A reviewer reading this would rightly ask: why not?
A strong landscape sentence:

Other theories in the field that address event cognition include the Event-Indexing Model and the Structure Building Framework. The Event-Indexing Model addresses when situational dimension changes trigger updating, but makes no prediction about detection latency as a function of predictability – it does not reach my dependent variable. The Structure Building Framework similarly specifies the conditions for substructure shifts but not their temporal dynamics.

Each exclusion has a specific reason, and each reason maps onto one of the two filters. You do not need to write this sentence in your paper. But you need to be able to write it before you write your paper. If a reviewer later asks why you did not discuss these theories, you have a precise, principled answer ready.
If you cannot give one of those two reasons for a theory’s exclusion, go back to the filters. The theory may belong in your paper after all.
If you cannot complete either sentence
Do not start writing. Talk to colleagues and collaborators first. Present your argument in a lab meeting. A theoretical framing that cannot be summarised in two sentences will not become clearer at 2000 words – it will become longer.

title: 2  Finding your Anchor
text:
2.4 The plain-language summary
Before you start writing, produce one more thing: a plain-language summary of your study. Write two or three sentences explaining what you are doing and why it matters, in language a non-specialist could understand. No jargon, no theory names, no technical terms for your dependent variable. If your study is about event boundaries and surprise ratings, write about how people experience moments of transition in everyday activities and what that tells us about how the mind works.
Think of it as a clarity test, not a dumbing-down exercise. If you cannot explain what your study is about without the technical vocabulary, the technical vocabulary may be doing more load-bearing work than it should – covering for an argument that is not yet fully clear.
Write it down and keep it. You will return to it in Chapter 5, where it serves a different purpose: not as a starting point, but as a check on whether six thousand words of academic writing have obscured what was clear before you began.




Kerr, N. L. (1998). HARKing: Hypothesizing After the Results are Known. Personality and Social Psychology Review, 2(3), 196–217. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327957pspr0203_4


Oberauer, K., & Lewandowsky, S. (2019). Addressing the theory crisis in psychology. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 26(5), 1596–1618. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-019-01645-2

title: 3  Two Filters, Three Outcomes
text:
3.1 Filter 1 (F1): Does it make a different prediction from your anchor?
Once you have your anchor theory, you will inevitably encounter other theories that feel relevant. Some of them are. Most of them are not — at least not for this paper.
The problem is that relevance is a spectrum, and “loosely related to the phenomenon I am studying” is not the same as “necessary for the argument I am making.” In a theory-rich field, almost any theory can be made to sound relevant with a sentence or two of framing. That is precisely the trap.
The two filters below force a more honest question: does this theory do any work in this paper? Not in the field generally. Not in a review article. In this paper, with this design, for this argument.
Apply them sequentially. A theory that fails the first filter does not need to be assessed against the second.
Not whether the theory addresses the same phenomenon — it almost certainly does, or you would not be considering it. The question is whether it predicts something different from your anchor, given your specific manipulation and dependent variable.
Two theories can address the same phenomenon at the field level while making identical predictions for your specific operationalization. Specificity of design is what creates the wedge — or reveals that there is none. A theory that makes no distinct prediction for your study is not a competitor. The reader should know it exists — hence the citation — but it has earned nothing more.
If the answer is no — citation only.
If the answer is yes — proceed to Filter 2.

title: 3  Two Filters, Three Outcomes
section: 3.2 Filter 2 (F2): Does it reach your dependent variable mechanistically?
text:
3.2 Filter 2 (F2): Does it reach your dependent variable mechanistically?
A theory can make different predictions in principle and still not connect to your specific measure. If the theory addresses the phenomenon at a level of description that does not reach your DV, it cannot be tested by your study — and a theory that cannot be tested by your study is not a live competitor in your argument.
Mentioning it briefly is the honest move: tell the reader the theory exists, that it addresses the phenomenon, and why your study does not test it.
If the answer is no — mention it and explain why it is not tested here.
If the answer is yes — you are in the territory of strong inference (Platt, 1964). Both theories earn full treatment. Your introduction sets up the competition explicitly. Your results deliver the verdict. Your discussion addresses what the outcome means for both accounts — including the one that lost.
The two filters produce three possible outcomes:



Outcome
Treatment




Fails F1
Citation only


Passes F1, fails F2
Mention + explain why not tested here


Passes both
Full treatment — strong inference






Two filters, three outcomes


The three outcomes determine not just whether a theory appears, but how it appears in your prose. Theories that failed F1 need only a citation. Theories that passed F1 but failed F2 earn one sentence, typically placed after you have stated your anchor theory:

Other theoretical accounts of [phenomenon] — including [Theory X] and [Theory Y] — offer complementary perspectives; the present study is specifically designed to test the prediction of [anchor theory] that [specific prediction].

This sentence signals awareness of the landscape without making the landscape your argument. If you feel the urge to write something more defensive — explaining at length why you excluded a theory — that is usually a sign that your anchor is not yet clearly enough stated. Strengthen the anchor first.






NoteWhat strong inference actually means



The term comes from a 1964 paper by the physicist John Platt, published in Science. Platt observed that some fields — molecular biology and high-energy physics in particular — were advancing far faster than others, and asked why. His answer was that the fast-moving fields systematically practiced a specific method: devising competing hypotheses, designing experiments whose outcomes could exclude at least one of them, and recycling the procedure with whatever hypotheses remained.
The key word is exclude. Strong inference is not about confirming your preferred theory. It is about designing a study that forces a decision. A result that is consistent with both theories is not strong inference, even if both theories were in play. Strong inference requires that at least one possible outcome of your study would count as evidence against at least one of the theories.
This is what makes Design B in the worked example a strong inference study: a slope of zero across dimension-change conditions would exclude the Event-Indexing Model’s additivity hypothesis; a positive slope would challenge EST’s global updating assumption. The design does not merely accommodate both theories. It puts them in genuine competition.
Platt was writing for scientists designing studies, not for writers revising manuscripts. For writers, strong inference is less a prescription than a diagnostic. It tells you what your study can legitimately claim about competing theories, and therefore how much space each theory has earned in your paper. If your design cannot adjudicate between two theories, the theories are not in genuine competition in this paper, and the writing should reflect that.
Platt’s practical test is worth carrying regardless: what experiment could disprove your hypothesis? A theory that has no answer to this question is not a scientific competitor, regardless of how relevant it feels.








TipWorked example: event cognition



The anchor theory in both designs below is Event Segmentation Theory (EST; Zacks et al. (2007)). EST holds that people continuously predict what will happen next based on an event model held in working memory. When prediction error rises, a boundary is perceived and the event model is updated.


3.2.1 Design A: Boundary detection latency
Participants watch short video clips in which the perceptual predictability of event boundaries is manipulated. The dependent variable is boundary detection latency — how quickly participants press a button when they perceive a boundary.
SPECT (Loschky et al., 2020)
SPECT integrates front-end perceptual processes — attentional selection and information extraction during fixations — with back-end event model construction. For a design manipulating perceptual predictability with detection latency as DV, SPECT and EST make the same prediction: detection is driven by mismatches between incoming perceptual input and the current event model. There is no distinct prediction. Fails F1. → Citation only.
Event-Indexing Model (Zwaan et al., 1995; Zwaan & Radvansky, 1998)
The Event-Indexing Model proposes that boundaries are perceived when discontinuities occur along situational dimensions — time, location, character, intention, and causation. EST attributes boundaries to perceptual prediction error; the Event-Indexing Model attributes them to dimensional discontinuity. In a design manipulating perceptual predictability, these can come apart: a boundary can involve high prediction error without a dimensional discontinuity, and vice versa. Passes F1.
The Event-Indexing Model specifies when situation model updating occurs, not how quickly. It makes no prediction about detection latency as a function of predictability. Fails F2. → Mention + explain why not tested here.
Structure Building Framework (Gernsbacher et al., 1990)
The Structure Building Framework predicts that boundaries arise from coherence breaks — points at which incoming information is sufficiently discrepant to trigger a shift to a new substructure. EST emphasizes perceptual prediction error; the Structure Building Framework emphasizes semantic coherence. These can come apart: a scene can involve high perceptual change without a coherence break, and vice versa. Passes F1.
The framework specifies what triggers a substructure shift, not how quickly one is detected. It makes no prediction about the temporal dynamics of boundary perception, and therefore does not reach detection latency as a dependent variable. Fails F2. → Mention + explain why not tested here.

3.2.1.1 How the filters apply in Design A



Theory
F1
F2
Treatment




SPECT
✗
—
Citation only


Event-Indexing Model
✓
✗
Mention + explain


Structure Building Framework
✓
✗
Mention + explain



No competing theory earns full treatment in this design. That is not a failure of scholarship — it accurately reflects what each candidate theory contributes to this specific argument.




3.2.2 Design B: Memory and prediction across dimension changes
Participants watch the same video clips, but now the number of situational dimension changes at event boundaries is manipulated — from one dimension changing to four. The dependent variables are recognition memory for boundary frames and prediction accuracy for events following the boundary (Huff et al., 2014).
Event-Indexing Model
EST and the Event-Indexing Model now make opposite predictions. EST holds that event model updating is global: the model is reset at every boundary regardless of how many dimensions change, so memory and prediction performance should be flat across conditions. The Event-Indexing Model holds that updating is incremental: only the dimensions that change are updated, so more dimension changes mean more updating effort. Memory performance should increase linearly with the number of dimension changes; prediction accuracy should decrease linearly.
Both theories address recognition memory and prediction accuracy directly and mechanistically. EST’s gating mechanism predicts deeper encoding at boundaries; the Event-Indexing Model’s additivity hypothesis predicts linear scaling with the number of dimension changes. Both theories reach both dependent variables. Passes F1 and F2.
→ Strong inference. The introduction sets up the competition between global and incremental updating. The results report whether the pattern across dimension-change conditions is flat or linear. The discussion addresses what the outcome means for both theories.

3.2.2.1 How the filters apply in Design B









Theory
F1
F2
Treatment




SPECT
✗
—
Citation only


Event-Indexing Model
✓
✓
Full treatment — strong inference


Structure Building Framework
✓
✗
Mention + explain



The same anchor, the same candidates, two different designs — and a fundamentally different argumentative structure. In Design A, the Event-Indexing Model earns a brief mention. In Design B, it earns equal billing. What changes is not the field, not the theories, but the design.




The next chapter addresses how to write the theoretical framing once you know which theories belong and at what level.




Gernsbacher, M. A., Varner, K. R., & Faust, M. E. (1990). Investigating differences in general comprehension skill. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 16(3), 430–445. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.16.3.430


Huff, M., Meitz, T. G. K., & Papenmeier, F. (2014). Changes in situation models modulate processes of event perception in audiovisual narratives. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 40(5), 1377–1388. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0036780


Loschky, L. C., Larson, A. M., Smith, T. J., & Magliano, J. P. (2020). The Scene Perception & Event Comprehension Theory (SPECT) Applied to Visual Narratives. Topics in Cognitive Science, 12, 311–351. https://doi.org/10.1111/tops.12455


Platt, J. R. (1964). Strong Inference. Science, 146(3642), 347–353. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.146.3642.347


Zacks, J. M., Speer, N. K., Swallow, K. M., Braver, T. S., & Reynolds, J. R. (2007). Event perception: A mind-brain perspective. Psychological Bulletin, 133(2), 273–293. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.133.2.273


Zwaan, R. A., Langston, M. C., & Graesser, A. C. (1995). The construction of situation models in narrative comprehension: An event-indexing model. Psychological Science, 6(5), 292–297. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1995.tb00513.x


Zwaan, R. A., & Radvansky, G. A. (1998). Situation models in language comprehension and memory. Psychological Bulletin, 123(2), 162–185. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.123.2.162

title: 4  Writing the Theoretical Framing
text:
4.1 The funnel structure
You have your anchor theory. You have run every other candidate through the two filters. You know what belongs in your paper and what does not. Now you have to write it.
A colleague once described writing as debugging your thinking. You believe the logic is clear until you try to put it into sentences, and then you discover the gaps. The funnel structure and the paragraph rule give the debugging process a shape.
Many students hit a second problem at this point. They understand the logic of what they want to say, but the prose comes out wrong: too dense, too hedged, or structured in a way that buries the argument. This chapter is about the writing itself — how the argument takes shape on the page.
A well-written theoretical framing moves from broad to narrow, from the phenomenon to the specific prediction. This is sometimes called the funnel structure, and it is standard for good reason: it orients the reader before it demands anything of them.
The funnel has three levels.

title: 4  Writing the Theoretical Framing
text:
The funnel structure for theoretical framing




4.1.1 The phenomenon
One or two sentences establishing what you are studying and why it matters. This is not a literature review. It is an orientation. The reader should finish these sentences knowing what the paper is about.


4.1.2 The anchor theory
A concise statement of the theoretical account that drives your hypotheses. What does the theory claim? What mechanism does it propose? What does it predict in general? Keep this tight, two to four sentences at most. You are not writing a review of the theory; you are establishing the lens through which your results will be interpreted.


4.1.3 The specific prediction
The narrowest point of the funnel. Given your anchor theory and your specific design, what do you predict and why? This sentence, or two at most, is the payoff of the entire theoretical framing. Everything before it exists to make this prediction feel inevitable.
For the prediction to land, the reader needs two things: to understand what the competing theories predict, and to see what gap your study fills. These are not separate sections of the framing — they are part of making the prediction feel inevitable. A prediction that appears without context feels asserted rather than argued. Competing theories and the gap belong inside the funnel, in the paragraphs that lead to the prediction.

title: 4  Writing the Theoretical Framing
text:
4.2 One paragraph, one job
The funnel describes the shape of the argument. The paragraph rule describes how that shape is built.
Each level of the funnel corresponds to a small number of paragraph jobs, and each paragraph should have exactly one of them. State that job in a single sentence before you write the paragraph. If you cannot state it, you are not ready to write the paragraph.
The jobs map onto the funnel levels as follows:
Phenomenon - Establish why the phenomenon matters
Anchor theory - Introduce the anchor theory and its core claim - State the specific prediction that follows from the anchor theory for your study
Specific prediction - Acknowledge competing theories and explain why your study does not test them - State the gap your study fills
That is five paragraph jobs for an entire theoretical framing section. The list follows directly from the funnel. A sixth or seventh paragraph means either a job has been split across paragraphs unnecessarily, or a paragraph has been added that the funnel does not require. In either case, go back to the filters and ask whether the additional paragraph is doing argumentative work or just adding coverage.
The most common structural mistake is the omnibus paragraph — a single paragraph that introduces a theory, summarises its history, notes its limitations, compares it to alternatives, and mentions your study without connecting it to the argument. The reader finishes it without knowing what to think. Keeping one job per paragraph prevents this.
The next chapter addresses what to do with the theories that did not make it into the funnel.

title: 5  Checking your argument
text:
5.1 The argument progression test
You have your funnel. You know what your anchor theory is and what it predicts. Your paper sits in a field full of other theories, and you need to handle them without letting them take over. The two-sentence check in the previous chapter gave you a principled basis for those decisions before you started writing. This chapter gives you three tests to apply once you have a draft.
A well-written theoretical framing is a sequence of steps, each of which advances the argument by exactly one move. For each paragraph in your theoretical framing, ask: what single step does this paragraph add to my argument? Write it down in one sentence. If you cannot, the paragraph needs to be revised or removed.
Then read your one-sentence summaries in sequence. They should tell the complete story of your theoretical argument – from phenomenon to prediction – without gaps or redundancy. A gap means a step is missing. Redundancy means two paragraphs are doing the same job, and one of them should go.
This is the paragraph-level version of the anchor sentence. The anchor sentence compresses your entire theoretical commitment into one sentence. The argument progression test checks whether every paragraph is doing real work on the way to that sentence.

title: 5  Checking your argument
text:
5.2 The elevator abstract
Once the argument holds paragraph by paragraph, try to compress the whole paper into two sentences: one stating what you did and why, one stating what you found and what it means. Do not open your manuscript while you do this. A contribution that cannot be stated in two sentences without opening the manuscript is not yet clear enough to publish.
A weak elevator abstract:

We studied event boundaries and surprise ratings in video clips. We found that surprise was lower at boundaries, which is consistent with Event Segmentation Theory.

A strong elevator abstract:

We tested the prediction of Event Segmentation Theory that boundary detection reduces prediction error, using surprise ratings as a direct measure of that error across boundary and within-event pause points in everyday activity clips. Surprise was reliably lower at boundaries, supporting the specific claim that event model updating – not narrative goal structure – drives the reduction.

title: 5  Checking your argument
text:
5.3 The plain-language summary
In Chapter 2, you produced a plain-language summary before writing. Return to it now and read it alongside your draft.
The two will sound different because a scientific paper and a plain-language summary are different documents. What matters is whether they are about the same thing. Academic writing accumulates qualifications, hedges, and theoretical scaffolding until the original point is no longer visible. If your plain-language summary describes a study that your draft no longer clearly resembles, the draft has drifted.
The plain-language summary does not change. The draft does.

title: 6  Theory in the Discussion
text:
6.1 Two jobs the Discussion actually has
The introduction is where theory overload is most visible. The Discussion is where it does its quieter damage. By the time the reader reaches it, they have invested in your argument, and a Discussion that loses focus is harder to recover from than an introduction that does.
The pattern is predictable. A writer who mentioned four theories in the introduction feels obliged to return to all four in the Discussion. The results came out in favour of Event Segmentation Theory, so that gets a paragraph. Then, because the Event-Indexing Model was mentioned in the introduction, it gets a sentence noting that the results are “also consistent with” it. The Structure Building Framework gets something similar. Narrative Comprehension accounts are noted as offering a “complementary perspective.” By the end, every theory that appeared in the introduction has reappeared in the Discussion, and the reader is no longer sure which one the paper was actually about.
This is the Discussion version of the omnibus paragraph. It has a specific cause: writers confuse two distinct tasks — interpreting results and cataloguing them.
The Discussion has two theoretical jobs, and only two.
Job 1: Explain what the results mean for your anchor theory. Did the results confirm the prediction? If so, what does that tell us about the mechanism? If not — or not cleanly — what does that tell us? This is the primary job. It should take most of the theoretical space in your Discussion.
Job 2: Address theories that passed both filters. If a competing theory earned full treatment in your introduction — because it made a different prediction that your design could adjudicate — then your results say something about it, and the Discussion should say what. Did your results discriminate between the theories? If yes, state that clearly. If no — if the results were consistent with both — say that too, and explain what a future study would need to look like to adjudicate them.
That is it. Two jobs. A theory that received only a brief mention in the introduction — because it failed F2 — has not earned a paragraph in the Discussion. Results being “consistent with” a theory is not a reason to discuss it — almost any result is consistent with almost any sufficiently flexible theory. “Consistent with” is not an argument.

title: 6  Theory in the Discussion
text:
6.2 The mirror test
Here is a simple diagnostic. Draw a line down the middle of a page. On the left, list every theory that received substantive treatment in your introduction. On the right, list every theory that receives substantive treatment in your Discussion.
The two lists should match. A theory that appears on the right but not the left has entered your paper through the back door — included in the Discussion without having earned its place in the introduction. A theory that appears on the left but not the right is a loose thread you left for the reader to trip on.
The mirror test does not mean every theory gets equal space in both sections. Your anchor theory will receive more Discussion than any competing theory. But every theory that earned full treatment in the introduction should receive at least a sentence in the Discussion, and no theory should appear in the Discussion without having appeared in the introduction.

title: 6  Theory in the Discussion
text:
6.3 What “consistent with” actually means
When writers say their results are “consistent with” Theory X, they usually mean one of three things: the theory makes no differential prediction for this design; both theories happen to predict the same outcome; or the theory is flexible enough to accommodate almost any result. In the first two cases, “consistent with” is true but uninformative. In the third, it is not even a meaningful claim.
The only case where “consistent with” earns space is when you can follow it with a sentence explaining why the consistency matters — what it adds to the argument that is not already provided by your anchor theory’s confirmation. If you cannot write that sentence, cut the phrase.

title: 6  Theory in the Discussion
text:
6.4 A before and after
Here is the Discussion opening from the bloated first attempt in Chapter 9:

The present results are consistent with Event Segmentation Theory, as predicted. Participants rated surprise as lower at event boundaries than within events, supporting the claim that boundary detection reduces prediction error. These results are also consistent with predictive processing frameworks, which similarly predict reduced prediction error at moments of perceptual transition. Situation Models offer a complementary perspective: the updating of spatial and causal representations at boundaries may contribute to the reduction in surprise we observed. Narrative Comprehension accounts suggest that goal-structure processing at event transitions could additionally account for the boundary effect.

Four theories in five sentences. The anchor result is buried under three sentences of “also consistent with.” The reader cannot tell which confirmation the paper is actually claiming.
Here is the same opening after applying the two-job rule:

Surprise ratings were lower at event boundaries than within events, confirming the prediction of Event Segmentation Theory that boundary detection triggers a model update that reduces prediction error. The effect was robust across all three activity types and held at both the group and individual level.
The present design does not discriminate between EST and predictive processing frameworks, which generate identical directional predictions for this paradigm. What the data do support is the more specific claim within EST that boundary-related prediction error reduction is perceptual rather than narrative in origin: the effect was present for clips of non-goal-directed activities as well as goal-directed ones, which is harder to account for on a goal-structure account.

Two paragraphs. The first states the confirmed prediction and its scope. The second does genuine theoretical work — it uses a feature of the results to narrow the theoretical picture. Theories that received only a brief mention in the introduction do not reappear here. The reader does not miss them.
The same logic that governed your introduction governs your Discussion. The anchor holds at both ends. The next chapter addresses what happens when a reviewer disagrees.

title: 7  Surviving Peer Review
text:
7.1 Why reviewers ask for more theory
You have written a focused, well-argued paper. You have your anchor theory. You have applied the two filters. You have a clear funnel. Then the reviews come back, and Reviewer 2 wants you to discuss three more theories.
This chapter is about how to handle that — calmly, professionally, and without abandoning the argument you worked hard to build.
It helps to understand why this happens before deciding how to respond.
Reviewers are experts in the field. They have spent years working with specific theoretical frameworks, and they notice when those frameworks are not discussed. This is not always unreasonable — sometimes a theory genuinely is relevant and the author missed it. But sometimes the request reflects habit, territory, or a different view of what a paper should do rather than a genuine gap in the argument.
The key question is simply to run the theory through the two filters: does it make a different prediction for this design? Does it offer a mechanistic account of this dependent variable?
If the answer is no to both, you have a principled basis for a polite but firm response. If the answer is yes to either, the reviewer may have identified something genuinely important — and you should take it seriously.

title: 7  Surviving Peer Review
section: 7.2 The two types of reviewer request
text:
7.2 The two types of reviewer request
Not all requests for more theory are the same. It helps to distinguish them.
The legitimate request. The reviewer identifies a theory that makes a differential prediction for your design and addresses your specific dependent variable. This is a gift. Engage with it seriously, run it through the filters, and if it passes both, include it properly — not as a defensive gesture, but as a genuine improvement to the argument.
The territorial request. The reviewer wants their preferred framework acknowledged, but the framework does not do any argumentative work in your paper. This is common. It does not mean the reviewer is acting in bad faith — it may simply reflect a different view of what thorough scholarship looks like. But agreeing to add a paragraph that does no argumentative work makes your paper worse.
The challenge is that you cannot always tell which type you are dealing with from the review alone. Start by assuming good faith and engaging seriously with the request before deciding how to respond.

title: 7  Surviving Peer Review
text:
7.3 How to respond
When a reviewer asks for more theoretical coverage, your response should do three things: acknowledge the framework they have raised, explain its relationship to your study specifically, and state clearly what role it plays in your revised manuscript.
A weak response:

We thank the reviewer for this suggestion. We have added a discussion of [Theory X] to the introduction.

This response adds words without adding argument. It is the written equivalent of the bloated manuscript you were trying to avoid.
A strong response:

We thank the reviewer for raising [Theory X]. This framework offers an important account of [phenomenon] at the level of [description]. In our study, however, [Theory X] and our anchor theory [Theory Y] generate identical predictions for [specific manipulation and DV], so including a substantive discussion of [Theory X] would not help the reader distinguish between the two accounts on the basis of our data. We have added a sentence acknowledging [Theory X] and its relationship to our theoretical framing [cite location in manuscript].

This response is respectful, specific, and principled. It shows the reviewer that you have engaged seriously with their request and explains clearly why a one-sentence acknowledgement is the appropriate level of treatment.

title: 7  Surviving Peer Review
text:
7.4 When to give ground
Sometimes the right answer is to give ground — not because the reviewer is right about the theory being essential, but because the cost of resistance is higher than the cost of inclusion. This is a judgment call, and it depends on the journal, the editor, and how central the paper is to your research programme.
If you do add a theory under pressure, add it properly. One weak paragraph that mentions a theory without engaging with it satisfies nobody and weakens the paper. If the theory is going in, apply the framework: does it make a different prediction for your design? Does it reach your dependent variable? What does including it add to the interpretation?
A theory that earns its place even under pressure is better than a theory that was always there for the wrong reasons.

title: 7  Surviving Peer Review
text:
7.5 A note on tone
Reviewer responses are read by editors as well as reviewers. A response that comes across as dismissive or impatient — even when you are right — can damage your chances with the editor. Firmness and respect are not opposites. The strong response above is firm precisely because it is specific and engaged, not because it is blunt.
When in doubt, err on the side of explaining more rather than less. A reviewer who feels heard is more likely to accept a principled disagreement than one who feels dismissed.
Chapter 8 addresses the practical mechanics of the revision process — the cover letter, the response document, and track changes.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.1 Before you write anything: the revision strategy
Getting reviews back is one of the most disorienting experiences in academic life. Even positive reviews require substantial work, and negative ones can feel like a personal attack on months or years of effort. This chapter is about the practical mechanics of turning a reviewed manuscript into an accepted one, with particular attention to the revision decisions that involve theoretical framing, which are consistently the most consequential and the hardest to get right.
The worst thing you can do when reviews arrive is open your manuscript and start making changes immediately. Before you touch a single word, you need a strategy.
Wait at least 24 hours. Reviews received on a bad day look different read on a good one. Decisions made in the first hour after a rejection are rarely the right ones.
Then read all the reviews once without taking notes. Read them again and sort every comment into three categories.
Must do. Changes that are clearly correct, clearly improve the paper, or are required by the editor. Do these without argument.
Negotiate. Changes you disagree with but where the reviewer has a legitimate concern. These require a response that engages seriously with the concern while explaining your position.
Resist. Changes that would genuinely weaken the paper. The most common example in theory-rich fields is a request to add theoretical coverage that does no argumentative work — a theory that fails F1 or F2 but that a reviewer considers important. These requests require a polite, principled, specific response: name the filter the theory fails, explain why your design cannot test it, and offer a citation as the appropriate level of treatment. Chapter 7 covers this in detail. A response that simply refuses without engaging the reviewer’s concern rarely lands well; a response that explains precisely why the addition would weaken rather than strengthen the argument usually does.
Do this sorting before you draft anything. It gives you a clear picture of the work ahead and prevents the common mistake of making unnecessary concessions out of anxiety.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.2 The cover letter
The cover letter is read by the editor, not the reviewers. Its job is to signal that you have taken the reviews seriously and done the work, and to flag anything the editor needs to know before reading the response document.
A good cover letter does four things.
States what the paper is. One sentence — the anchor sentence from your theoretical framing, adapted for a non-specialist editor. This is not the time for nuance; it is the time for clarity.
Acknowledges the reviewers’ main concerns. Two or three sentences summarising what the reviewers asked for. This shows the editor you read the reviews carefully and understood them.
States what you did. A brief summary of the major changes — not a list of every edit, but the substantive responses to the substantive concerns.
Notes any points of disagreement. If you have not complied with a reviewer request, say so here, briefly and professionally. Editors dislike surprises. A cover letter that flags a polite disagreement is far better than a response document the editor has to read to discover you ignored a reviewer.
A cover letter template:

Dear [Editor],
We are pleased to resubmit our manuscript “[Title]” for consideration in [Journal]. The paper [one-sentence statement of contribution].
We thank the reviewers for their careful reading. The reviews raised three main concerns: [brief summary]. We have addressed each of these in the revised manuscript.
The major changes are as follows: [two to four sentences summarising substantive changes]. We have also made numerous smaller revisions throughout to improve clarity.
We were unable to fully comply with one reviewer request: [brief statement]. Our reasons are explained in detail in the response document.
Sincerely, [Authors]

Keep it to one page. Editors are busy. A cover letter that runs to two pages has already made a bad impression.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.3 The response document
The response document is a formal record of every reviewer comment and your response to it. Done well, it makes the editor’s job easy. Done badly, it creates more work for everyone.
Structure. Organise by reviewer, then by comment. Number every comment and every response:

Reviewer 1, Comment 3:

The authors should discuss Event Segmentation Theory in relation to predictive processing frameworks.

Response:
We thank the reviewer for this suggestion. [Your response here.] The relevant change appears on p. 4, lines 67–69 of the revised manuscript.

Four rules for a strong response document:
Always quote the reviewer. Never paraphrase. Quoting shows you read the comment carefully and prevents misunderstandings.
Always give a page and line reference for every change you made. This allows the editor and reviewers to find your revisions without reading the entire manuscript.
Be specific about what you changed. “We have revised this section” is not a response. “We have added two sentences on p. 4 clarifying the relationship between our manipulation and the boundary detection measure” is a response.
For points you are not complying with, explain why clearly and specifically. Then offer something: an acknowledgement, a clarification, a sentence added. A pure refusal with no gesture towards the reviewer’s concern rarely lands well.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.4 Track changes
When submitting a revision, most journals ask for a version showing what was altered. Open the file you submitted, save a copy under a new filename such as paper_revision_1.docx, and enable track changes before writing a single word. Never revise the original — you need an untouched baseline in case a co-author or editor later asks what changed between rounds.
Be selective. Minor copyediting and formatting changes do not need to be visible — they create noise that obscures the substantive revisions. Track the things that matter: added paragraphs, deleted sections, changed conclusions, new analyses.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.5 Rejection and resubmission
A rejection is hard to receive but carries information. Read it carefully before deciding what to do next.
Desk rejection usually signals one of three things: the paper is outside the journal’s scope, the contribution is not seen as significant enough for that venue, or the writing did not communicate the contribution clearly enough. The first is a targeting problem. The second and third are fixable. Journal editors vary in how much they explain — some give you direction, some do not.
Rejection after review gives you something to work with. The reviews tell you exactly what needs to change. A rejection at one journal with a clear set of reviews is often an acceptance at another, if you take them seriously.
Before resubmitting elsewhere, ask honestly: do the reviews identify a genuine weakness in the argument or the evidence? If yes, fix it before resubmitting. Fields are small, and reviewers are not randomly distributed across journals — the person who reviewed your paper at one venue may well be asked to review it at the next. Do not send the same paper to a lower-ranked journal and hope the problems go unnoticed.
If the reviews reflect a scope mismatch rather than a genuine flaw, resubmit with confidence, but sharpen the cover letter to address the mismatch directly.

title: 8  The Revision Process
text:
8.6 A note on tone
Every document in the revision process is read by human beings who are doing you a favour. Reviewers are unpaid. Editors are overworked. A response that is clear, specific, and professionally warm makes their job easier and creates goodwill that carries forward.
This does not mean being sycophantic; everyone can tell when praise is hollow. It means being direct, being specific, and treating the process as a collaboration even when it does not feel like one.

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.1 The study
This chapter walks through the complete process — from choosing an anchor theory to handling a reviewer request and writing a cover letter — using a fictional but realistic study in event cognition. The study is invented, but the problems it illustrates are real.
Imagine you have conducted the following study:
This is a clean, focused study. It has one manipulation, one dependent variable, and one theoretical prediction. It should produce a clean, focused theoretical framing. It often does not — and here is why.

title: 9  A Worked Example
text: Participants watched short video clips of everyday activities — making coffee, unpacking groceries, setting a table. At unpredictable moments the video paused and participants rated their surprise on a 7-point scale. Pause points were either at event boundaries (moments of natural transition between sub-actions) or within events (mid-action). The dependent variable was surprise rating. The hypothesis was that surprise ratings would be lower at boundaries than within events, because boundary detection involves an updating of the current event model that reduces prediction error.

title: 9  A Worked Example
section: 9.2 The first attempt: what goes wrong
text:
9.2 The first attempt: what goes wrong
Here is how a typical first draft of the theoretical framing might look:

Event cognition has been studied from multiple theoretical perspectives. Event Segmentation Theory (Zacks et al., 2007) proposes that people continuously segment the stream of activity into discrete events, with boundaries occurring at points of maximal change. Situation Models (Zwaan & Radvansky, 1998) describe the mental representations that readers and observers construct during narrative comprehension, including spatial, temporal, and causal dimensions. Narrative Comprehension accounts (Graesser et al., 1994) emphasise the role of goal structures and causal chains in organising event understanding. More recently, predictive processing frameworks (Clark, 2013) have proposed that perception involves continuous prediction and error-correction at multiple levels of abstraction. Each of these frameworks offers important insights into how people understand and remember everyday events.
In the present study, we examined surprise ratings at event boundaries and within events.

This paragraph mentions four theories in five sentences and commits to none of them. The reader finishes it knowing that event cognition is complicated and that something was measured. They do not know what the study is for.
Notice also the final sentence — “we examined surprise ratings at event boundaries and within events” — which describes the method without stating the prediction. The theoretical framing has done no work.

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.3 Applying the framework
Before rewriting, let’s apply the tools from the previous chapters.
Step 1: The anchor exercise

My anchor theory is Event Segmentation Theory, which predicts that surprise ratings will be lower at event boundaries than within events in my study, because boundary detection triggers an updating of the current event model that reduces prediction error, making boundary moments feel less surprising than mid-event pauses.

This sentence is specific. It names a mechanism (event model updating), connects it to a specific outcome (lower surprise ratings), and could only describe this study.
Step 2: Running the two filters
Situation Models:

F1: Does it make a different prediction from EST for this design? No — both frameworks predict lower surprise at boundaries due to reduced prediction error. They differ in mechanism but not in direction. → Citation only.

Narrative Comprehension accounts:

F1: Does it make a different prediction for this design? No — these accounts address goal and causal structures in narrative, not perceptual boundary detection in video. → Citation only.

Predictive processing:

F1: Does it make a different prediction? In this design, no — both EST and predictive processing predict reduced prediction error at boundaries, producing the same directional prediction for surprise ratings. → Citation only, with one sentence acknowledging the connection.

The result: Event Segmentation Theory is the anchor. The other three frameworks get at most one sentence between them.

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.4 The revised framing
Here is what the theoretical framing looks like after applying the framework:

Everyday activities have a natural structure. Actions unfold in sequences — making coffee involves boiling water, then adding grounds, then pouring — and people are remarkably consistent in where they perceive one sub-action to end and another to begin (Zacks & Tversky, 2001). These perceived transitions are event boundaries, and they play a central role in Event Segmentation Theory (EST; Zacks et al., 2007).
EST proposes that people maintain an ongoing model of the current event — a working representation of what is happening and what is likely to happen next. At event boundaries, this model is updated: the current event ends, a new one begins, and prediction error is reduced. The theory predicts that boundary moments should therefore feel less surprising than within-event moments, where the current model is still active and prediction error may be higher.
The present study tests this prediction directly. Participants watched video clips of everyday activities and rated their surprise at pause points located either at event boundaries or within events. If EST is correct, surprise ratings should be lower at boundaries than within events. Other accounts of event cognition — including Situation Models and Narrative Comprehension frameworks — address complementary aspects of event understanding but do not generate differential predictions for surprise ratings as a function of boundary location in this design.

Three paragraphs. One job each. The first establishes the phenomenon and orients the reader. The second introduces the anchor theory and its mechanism. The third states the specific prediction and places the other frameworks in one sentence.

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.5 The reviews arrive
The paper is submitted and reviews come back. After waiting 24 hours, you sort the comments:
Must do:

Reviewer 1 asks for clearer reporting of effect sizes → correct and straightforward, add to results section
Reviewer 2 notes a missing reference for the boundary detection paradigm → correct, add the citation

Negotiate:

Reviewer 1 suggests the discussion overstates the implications for predictive processing → legitimate concern, revise the discussion to be more precise about scope

Resist:

Reviewer 2 requests a substantive discussion of predictive processing frameworks throughout the introduction → does not change the theoretical argument; one sentence already acknowledges it

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.6 The reviewer response

Reviewer 2, Comment 3:

The authors fail to engage with predictive processing frameworks, which are highly relevant to the phenomena under investigation. A thorough discussion of Clark (2013) and related work is needed.

Response:
We thank the reviewer for raising predictive processing frameworks. We agree that predictive processing offers an important and compatible account of boundary-related phenomena. However, in the present design, predictive processing and Event Segmentation Theory generate identical directional predictions for surprise ratings at boundaries versus within-event pause points — both frameworks predict lower surprise at boundaries due to reduced prediction error. Because our data cannot adjudicate between these accounts, a substantive discussion of predictive processing would imply a contrast our study is not designed to test. We have added a sentence in the introduction acknowledging the relationship between EST and predictive processing frameworks (p. 4, lines 67–69).

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.7 The cover letter

Dear [Editor],
We are pleased to resubmit our manuscript “Event boundaries reduce surprise: Evidence from a video-based rating task” for consideration in [Journal]. The paper tests the prediction of Event Segmentation Theory that surprise ratings will be lower at event boundaries than within events, using a video-based paradigm with everyday activities.
We thank the reviewers for their careful reading. The reviews raised three main concerns: the reporting of effect sizes, a missing reference, and the treatment of predictive processing frameworks. We have addressed the first two fully. On the third, we have added a sentence acknowledging the relationship between EST and predictive processing, but have not added a substantive discussion — our reasons are explained in detail in the response document.
The major changes are as follows: effect sizes have been added throughout the results section; the Zacks & Tversky (2001) reference has been added on p. 2; the discussion has been revised to more precisely scope the implications for predictive processing accounts; and a sentence has been added on p. 4 acknowledging the relationship between EST and predictive processing frameworks.
Sincerely, [Authors]

title: 9  A Worked Example
text:
9.8 What this example shows
The difference between the first attempt and the revised version is not a difference in knowledge. The writer of the first attempt knew all four theories. The difference is a decision — a commitment to one theoretical account and a willingness to let that commitment do the work.
The reviewer response and cover letter follow directly from that commitment. Because the theoretical framing is principled, the response to Reviewer 2 is principled. Because the argument is clear, the cover letter is clear. Everything depends on the anchor.
Everything else — the funnel, the two filters, the response document, the cover letter — follows from that commitment. The anchor is where the work begins.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.1 The expansion problem
AI writing tools are now part of academic life. Most PhD students and postdocs use them in some form — for drafting, for editing, for overcoming the paralysis of the blank page. This is not a problem. Used well, AI can make the writing process faster and less painful.
Used poorly, it makes exactly the problem this guide is about significantly worse.
AI writing tools have a systematic tendency to expand. Ask an AI to improve your introduction and it will suggest adding things — more context, more nuance, more theories (Uhler et al., 2026). This is not a bug in any individual tool; it reflects how these systems are trained. Large language models are typically fine-tuned using human feedback, where human raters judge which outputs are better. Raters tend to reward outputs that seem comprehensive and thorough — so models learn that adding content is usually the safer move. The result is a tool that is structurally biased towards inclusion.
In the AI research literature, a related phenomenon is called sycophancy: the tendency of models to tell users what seems agreeable rather than what is accurate or useful (Cheng et al., 2026). In the context of academic writing, both tendencies — expansion and sycophancy — produce exactly the bloat this guide is designed to prevent.
A concrete example. You have a focused theoretical framing — three paragraphs, one anchor theory, one prediction. You ask an AI to improve it. The AI returns five paragraphs. It has added a paragraph on Situation Models because they are “highly relevant to event cognition research.” It has added a sentence noting that “predictive processing frameworks offer a complementary perspective.” The writing is fluent. The argument is weaker.
The problem is invisible if you do not know what to look for. The expanded version sounds authoritative. It sounds thorough. It sounds like a paper that will satisfy reviewers. It is the first attempt from the worked example chapter, generated in thirty seconds.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.2 How expansion manifests
Sycophantic expansion produces three recognisable failure modes. They share a common root — the AI optimising for what seems agreeable rather than what is argumentatively sound — but they show up differently on the page.
Sycophantic expansion. AI adds theories, qualifications, and hedges that dilute the argument. This is the most common failure mode and the hardest to detect because the output looks polished.
Confident vagueness. AI connects theories to your argument with fluent but imprecise language. Phrases like “this aligns with”, “is consistent with”, and “offers a complementary perspective on” sound substantive but say nothing about whether the theory makes a differential prediction for your design. A theoretical framing full of these phrases fails the two filters — does this theory make a different prediction for my design, and does it reach my dependent variable mechanistically? — while appearing to pass them.
Premature polish. AI produces text that looks finished before the argument is sound. This kills the impulse to revise. If a paragraph reads well, it feels wrong to delete it — even if it is doing no argumentative work. The polish is a trap.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.3 What AI does well
Once you understand the expansion problem, the question is not whether to use AI but how to use it against its own tendencies. Before that, three genuinely unproblematic uses:

Sentence-level editing — improving clarity, flow, and concision once the argument is already clear
Formatting and consistency — standardising terminology, checking reference formatting, fixing grammar
Overcoming blank page paralysis — generating a rough first draft that you then revise heavily

For everything else — drafting theoretical framings, cutting redundancy, checking argument structure, compressing the paper, stress-testing against a critical reviewer — the prompts in the next section show how to use AI productively by giving it the right constraints.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.4 How to use AI well
The most important rule: complete your anchor sentence and your two-sentence check before opening an AI tool. These are the constraints that keep AI expansion in check. Without them, the AI has no basis for knowing what belongs in your paper and what does not — and it will optimise for comprehensiveness, which is the opposite of what you need.
With those constraints in place, AI can be used productively at every stage of the writing process.

10.4.1 Before you write
For drafting:

I am writing the theoretical framing for a study that tests the following prediction: [paste anchor sentence]. Write a three-paragraph introduction that moves from the phenomenon to the anchor theory to this specific prediction. Do not introduce any theoretical frameworks other than [anchor theory].



10.4.2 Once you have a draft
The following prompts work on existing text. In each case, the anchor sentence is the constraint that stops the AI from optimising for comprehensiveness instead of argumentative clarity.
For cutting:

Here is my theoretical framing: [paste text]. My anchor sentence is: [paste anchor sentence]. Identify every sentence that does not directly serve this argument and suggest removing it.

For the argument progression test:

Here is my theoretical framing: [paste text]. For each paragraph, state in one sentence what single step it adds to the argument. Then tell me whether the sequence of steps tells a complete story from phenomenon to prediction, and identify any gaps or redundancies.

For the elevator abstract:

Here is my full manuscript: [paste text]. Write a two-sentence summary: one sentence stating what I did and why, one sentence stating what I found and what it means. Use the language of the manuscript but no more jargon than is necessary.

For the plain-language summary:

Here is my theoretical framing: [paste text]. Explain what this study is about and why it matters in two or three sentences, using no technical terms and no theory names. Write it for someone with no background in psychology.

For critical review:

Here is my theoretical framing: [paste text]. My anchor sentence is: [paste anchor sentence]. Act as a critical reviewer. For each theory that receives more than a citation, assess whether it passes both filters: does it make a different prediction from my anchor for this specific design, and does it reach my dependent variable mechanistically? Flag any theory that receives substantive treatment without passing both filters. Flag any sentence that uses the phrases “consistent with”, “aligns with”, or “offers a complementary perspective” without specifying a differential prediction.

The critical review prompt is the sharpest of these tools: it turns the sycophantic tendency on its head by explicitly instructing the AI to look for the weaknesses that sycophancy would normally conceal.
If the critical review prompt flags weaknesses in your theoretical framing, address them before submission. The next section deals with the harder case: a reviewer who flags them after.


10.4.3 After reviews come back
For reviewer responses:

A reviewer has asked me to add a discussion of [Theory X]. My anchor theory is [Theory Y]. In my design, both theories predict [same outcome]. Write a polite reviewer response explaining why a one-sentence acknowledgement is the appropriate level of treatment.

Notice the pattern across all these prompts: in every case, you give the AI a constraint derived from the tools in this guide. The argument progression test prompt asks AI to do what you would do manually — but faster, and without the attachment to your own prose that makes self-editing hard. The plain-language summary prompt uses AI as a stand-in for the non-specialist reader: if the output does not match what you wrote before drafting, the draft has drifted from its original clarity. These prompts turn AI’s tendencies to compress and summarise into assets rather than liabilities — the opposite of asking it to expand and improve.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.5 A note on honesty
Using AI to assist with writing is not dishonest, provided you follow your institution’s guidelines and disclose appropriately. But there is a subtler form of dishonesty worth naming: using AI to generate a theoretical framing you do not fully understand, because the framing sounds authoritative enough to submit.
If you cannot explain in your own words why each theory in your introduction is there — what it predicts for your design, why it matters for your argument — then the framing is not yours, regardless of who edited the sentences. A reviewer who asks a sharp question about your theoretical choices will expose this immediately.
AI can help you write what you think. It cannot think for you. Every tool in this guide — the anchor exercise, the two filters, the two-sentence check, the argument progression test, the elevator abstract, the plain-language summary — is a thinking tool. Do it yourself, on paper, before you open the AI.

title: 10  Writing with AI
text:
10.6 The sharp tool
A sharp tool cuts well in the right hands and badly in the wrong ones. The same AI prompt produces a focused, well-argued theoretical framing when the writer has a clear anchor — and a bloated, theory-laden paragraph when they do not.
The guide you have just read is, among other things, a set of instructions for how to pick up the tool correctly. The anchor sentence is the handle. The constraints you bring to the tool determine how precisely it cuts.




Cheng, M., Lee, C., Khadpe, P., Yu, S., Han, D., & Jurafsky, D. (2026). Sycophantic AI decreases prosocial intentions and promotes dependence. Science, 391(6792), eaec8352. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aec8352


Uhler, L., Jordan, V., Buder, J., Huff, M., & Papenmeier, F. (2026). Influence of solution efficiency and valence of instruction on additive and subtractive solution strategies in humans, GPT-4, and GPT-4o. Communications Psychology, 4(1), 41. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44271-026-00403-0

title: Further Reading
text:
On writing and structure
This guide deliberately keeps its reference list short. The following are the books, papers, and theses most worth reading alongside it — selected for usefulness to PhD students and postdocs, not for comprehensiveness.
Mensh, B., & Kording, K. (2017). Ten simple rules for structuring papers. PLOS Computational Biology, 13(9), e1005619. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1005619
The paper that most directly inspired this guide. Concise, practical, and applicable across disciplines. Read it before writing your first manuscript and again before submitting your first revision.
Mensh and Kording address the structure of a paper as a whole — abstract, introduction, results, discussion — at the level of sections, paragraphs, and sentences. This guide addresses a narrower problem they deliberately set aside: how to decide which theoretical content belongs in the paper in the first place, and how to write it without letting the theoretical landscape swallow the argument. The two are complementary. If you find yourself asking how to structure what you have decided to include, Mensh and Kording is the next thing to read.
Schimel, J. (2012). Writing science: How to write papers that get cited and proposals that get funded. Oxford University Press.
The best full-length book on scientific writing for researchers. Particularly strong on narrative structure and why papers fail to communicate. Recommended for anyone who wants to go deeper than this guide.
Pinker, S. (2014). The sense of style: The thinking person’s guide to writing in the 21st century. Viking.
Not specifically about academic writing, but the best book on clear prose available. The chapter on the curse of knowledge is essential reading for anyone who has ever been told their writing is too technical.

title: Further Reading
text:
On theory in psychology
Meehl, P. E. (1978). Theoretical risks and tabular asterisks: Sir Karl, Sir Ronald, and the slow progress of soft psychology. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 46(4), 806–834. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-006X.46.4.806
A foundational paper on why psychological theories are hard to test and why the field accumulates theories faster than it retires them. Difficult but worth the effort.
Oberauer, K., & Lewandowsky, S. (2019). Addressing the theory crisis in psychology. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 26(5), 1596–1618. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-019-01645-2
A more recent and accessible treatment of the same problem. Directly relevant to the argument in Chapter 1 of this guide.
McPhetres, J., Albayrak-Aydemir, N., Barbosa Mendes, A., et al. (2021). A decade of theory as reflected in Psychological Science (2009–2019). PLOS ONE, 16(3), e0247986. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0247986
Empirical evidence for the theory inflation problem. Across 2,225 articles in Psychological Science, the authors identified 359 distinct theories mentioned in text — most referred to only once. Theories proliferate faster than they are tested or retired.
Platt, J. R. (1964). Strong inference. Science, 146(3642), 347–353. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.146.3642.347
Platt observed that fields advancing quickly shared a specific habit: devising competing hypotheses and designing experiments that could exclude at least one of them. Written for bench scientists in 1964, but the core question — what result would count against your hypothesis? — applies directly to the problem of theoretical framing in psychology.

title: Further Reading
text:
On the review process
Sternberg, R. J. (2002). On civility in reviewing. APS Observer. https://www.psychologicalscience.org/observer/on-civility-in-reviewing
Short and direct. Required reading before responding to your first hostile review.

title: Further Reading
text:
On writing with AI
Cheng, M., Lee, C., Khadpe, P., Yu, S., Han, D., & Jurafsky, D. (2026). Sycophantic AI decreases prosocial intentions and promotes dependence. Science, 391, eaec8352. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aec8352
The empirical basis for the sycophancy problem described in Chapter 10. Across 11 state-of-the-art models, AI affirmed users’ positions nearly 50% more often than humans — even when those positions involved deception or harm.
Uhler, L., Jordan, V., Buder, J., Huff, M., & Papenmeier, F. (2026). Influence of solution efficiency and valence of instruction on additive and subtractive solution strategies in humans, GPT-4, and GPT-4o. Communications Psychology, 5(1), 41. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44271-026-00403-0
Shows that AI tools systematically favour additive over subtractive solutions — directly relevant to why AI expands theoretical framings rather than cutting them.

Suggestions for additions to this list are welcome — contact markus.huff@uni-tuebingen.de.
